data.title: Global Macro Outlook: Fourth Quarter 2025
data.description:
The Macro Picture

After an uncertain economic policy outlook dominated the first half of the year, the third quarter brought more clarity. The tariff regime in the United States appears largely set at this point, the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act solidified fiscal policy for the time being, and the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) answered the question about when it would cut rates by doing so in September. To be clear, the environment remains somewhat uncertain - as is always the case - but the tail risks around our central scenario for the next few quarters have diminished meaningfully of late. Financial markets have responded to the reduction in uncertainty with a period of relatively low volatility, as one would expect. From a cyclical perspective, that seems likely to persist for the next few months.

That said, we are growing increasingly concerned about longer-term, structural fragilities in the system. On top of obvious fiscal vulnerabilities throughout the developed world and questions about the role of the dollar, the threat to central bank independence in the US is another issue with potentially massive long-term implications for the economy and financial markets. For now, markets appear content to ignore the long-term risks and to focus on the near term, but the fuel for future crises continues to accumulate. Investors should remember that when crises come, they tend to arrive suddenly and without advance warning.

In the meantime, however, the global economy continues to proceed along the lines we have described in past outlooks. Economic policy changes remain unlikely to trigger a disruptive global recession; Indeed, with the tariff regime now appearing largely settled, we anticipate some improvement in business sentiment as uncertainty fades. Still, even as uncertainty wanes, economic actors are now left to wrestle with the tangible impact of tariffs. Growth has already slowed throughout the developed world and will continue to do so, we expect, as businesses face the reality of higher input costs and consumers the reality of higher prices.

It has taken somewhat longer for the impact of tariffs to be felt in the US than we originally expected, primarily because the corporate sector was able to front-load its overseas purchases ahead of the policy changes. With those inventories now largely exhausted, we are seeing an impact on operations. Hiring has slowed sharply as businesses brace for a higher cost structure. At the same time, some price pressures are passing through to consumers, which has slowed final domestic demand. If slower hiring turns into layoffs in the coming months, the modest slowdown underway could spiral into something more pernicious.

That is not our base case, however. We expect that businesses will be able to slog through a few quarters of sluggish demand without having to cut their workforces meaningfully, and we believe that consumers in aggregate have the ability to withstand higher prices as long as they remain employed. While it’s true that the rate of hiring has slowed, we believe that focusing only on hiring likely overstates the weakness in the labor market. Migration and deportation policy represent a negative supply shock to the workforce: there are simply fewer available workers. As a result, the number of new hires required every month to employ new entrants to the labor force has declined. The run rate of hiring, then, is simultaneously well below long-term averages and also likely sufficient to keep the labor market afloat.

While growth is slowing, prices are rising. Goods prices in the US have risen markedly since tariffs were announced in April, and we expect more to come as businesses pass tariff costs through to consumers. To be clear, we do not expect inflation to reach the lofty heights of the post-pandemic period, and the stability of inflation expectations suggests that most others don’t either. That’s good news because it means that the Fed can continue to cut interest rates to prevent the labor market from weakening more seriously. That should limit the magnitude of the economic slowdown underway and pave the way for growth to pick back up over the course of 2026. We expect the Fed to cut rates somewhat more rapidly than the market is currently priced for and anticipate that the policy rate will fall below three percent this cycle.

Of course, the policy rate is not the most significant part of the interest-rate curve. Longer-dated rates are more relevant to the economy than short-term rates; it is long-term rates that affect everything from corporate borrowing to mortgage rates. And the behavior of long-term rates is uncertain, even if the Fed continues to cut the policy rate. If the market believes that cutting rates is cyclically appropriate, then long-term rates should be stable or somewhat lower. If, however, lower policy rates appear to be the result of political pressure or if they reflect a loss of central bank independence, then longer-date rates might rise as the Fed cuts. For now, we expect rates to remain contained, but with the Fed’s independence clearly under attack from the executive branch, the risk of a breakdown in the bond market is rising. If the Fed does lose independence, an unwieldy rise in yields would become a matter of when, not if. Central bank independence is a key underpinning of the entire financial system, and threats to that independence risk broader chaos. While markets remain focused on the near term, the longer-term story is likely to be much more significant.

Away from the US, central banks are well advanced in their efforts to mitigate the impact of tariffs on the global economy. Policymakers have lowered rates throughout Europe and in most emerging markets. We noted last quarter that the combination of policy easing and a final tariff regime that was less onerous than the one initially announced in early April would likely make the global slowdown moderate rather than severe, and so far that seems to be the case. We have modestly upgraded our forecasts for European growth in 2025, albeit largely for reasons unrelated to domestic demand, and anticipate an inflection toward more rapid growth next year.

Emerging-market central banks have already cut rates significantly as well, and local currency strength against the dollar has kept the door open for more easing in many countries. A slow global economy, but one that avoids recession, seems likely to benefit emerging markets, which offer higher yields than most of the developed world. Trade policy remains a risk of course, and many countries find themselves caught in an uncomfortable bind in measuring their relationships with the US and China. Different countries are likely to approach the situation in different ways based on both geopolitics and economics.

On the economic front, China’s trajectory remains somewhat idiosyncratic. Deflation remains a real risk and policy measures taken to date have only stabilized growth and prices rather than pushing them higher. We expect Chinese authorities to remain cautious until they are confident in the path forward for the relationship between China’s economy and that of the US, which means we do not expect a significant turn higher for Chinese growth in the near term. Housing markets remain a key pressure point, and relief there remains some distance in the future. For now, China’s economy will have to be content with simply muddling through.

What are markets to make of the environment? The near term looks decent enough. Economic performance is unlikely to be exciting, but it is also unlikely to be catastrophic. There are good reasons to expect growth to pick up in a few quarters, albeit to a lower average rate than we have become accustomed to owing to diminished global trade. And recent market performance, even in a volatile political environment, has given investors comfort that markets can withstand political and policy turbulence. At the same time, however, longer-term fragilities and frictions are growing rapidly and have the potential to be very disruptive with little advance warning.

Meantime, markets appear content to focus on the short term and leave the long term for later. That is a reasonable position to take: as long as the music is playing, it is probably best to keep dancing. But history teaches us that when the music stops, it’s often without warning. Complacency can be dangerous and even periods of strong market performance like that of the third quarter can give way very quickly to volatility. We cannot predict when that will happen, unfortunately, but investors should be prepared to act quickly when it does: under the market’s calm surface, the potential causes of the next crisis are bubbling faster as time passes.

Global Macro Outlook: The Next Six Months

Global Forecast

Forecast Overview

Key Assumptions

  • Financial: We assume that financial conditions will not tighten in a way that disrupts the outlook.
  • Geopolitical: We assume persistent geopolitical tension but also expect it will not meaningfully impact the global economy or markets.
  • Monetary Policy: Global central banks are likely to continue cutting rates to one degree or another over the next few quarters.

Central Narrative

  • Global Growth: While trade policy uncertainty has diminished, the direct impact of tariffs will slow growth.
  • Inflation: Stable inflation expectations are an important anchor and should keep medium-term inflation contained.
  • Yields: We generally expect stability in longer-term rates, after the decline earlier in the year.
  • USD: We expect additional dollar weakness, though at a slower pace than has been the case this year so far.

Key Upside Risks

  • Central bank easing could boost investment and growth.
  • Renewed business investment, as uncertainty has faded, would be a positive indication of future growth.

Key Downside Risks

  • The direct impact of tariffs has yet to be fully felt.
  • Threats to Fed independence could prompt disorderly moves in the Treasury market.
  • Market complacency suggests some vulnerability in the event of a shock.

  • Growth has slowed over the course of the year and is on pace to be well below the growth rate of the last few years. Still, it’s likely to remain positive - an environment that continues to look like a soft landing.
  • The labor market is in flux. Demand for workers has deteriorated as growth has slowed, but migration and deportation policy has reduced labor supply almost in parallel. That has left the labor market in a curious equilibrium in which slow hiring by historical standards may be sufficient to absorb the much slower growth rate of the available workforce. If sustained, this equilibrium would lead to lower growth over time.
  • Inflation is a mixed bag. Goods prices are clearly being impacted by tariffs and are moving higher, while shelter inflation continues to decelerate. Overall inflation is above the Fed’s target and unlikely to fall to that level over a reasonable forecast horizon; how the Fed responds to that systematic miss will be a very interesting question in the years to come.

Risk Factors

  • Markets have responded favorably to increased confidence in the policy regime. If policy volatility resurfaces, market complacency could be shaken.
  • Inflation expectations have been stable. If they move higher as tariffs increasingly filter into the economy, that will limit the Fed’s flexibility to cut rates and support a weaker labor market.
  • The biggest risks are structural rather than cyclical. Attacks on the Fed’s independence threaten its ability to foster a stable economy. History teaches that central banks without independence are ineffective at keeping inflation subdued.

Overview

What one sees when assessing the US economic outlook depends almost entirely on the time horizon. From a cyclical perspective, the next few quarters seem likely to be somewhat stable - slow but positive growth, moderate price pressures and gradually lower interest rates. From a structural perspective, however, the longer-term outlook is deteriorating significantly. Imbalances are large and growing, and policymakers’ ability to respond appropriately to those imbalances is increasingly subject to doubt.

First the cyclical: resolution to trade negotiations has reduced uncertainty and should pave the way for business investment to bridge at least some of the economic gap created as consumers pull back in the face of tariff-induced inflation. That inflation should be short-lived, and our expectation is that growth will pick up in 2026 as price pressures fade. The labor market is the key variable to monitor. Our assumption is that businesses will not pivot from slow hiring to significant layoffs; that should allow consumers to muddle through for the next few quarters. If we are wrong and layoffs mount, the situation will look very different, but that is not the base case. Instead, the labor market is in a strange equilibrium in which slower demand for workers is being met by less supply: immigration and deportation policy have reduced the workforce in a way that has kept the labor market more or less balanced even as hiring has slowed sharply. That equilibrium may prove temporary, but to the extent it is durable, it suggests that growth will be slower in the future than in the past (fewer workers = less growth). Still, less growth is not the same as no growth, and we continue to view a recession as unlikely.

Even as the near-term outlook looks relatively stable, however, structural developments are alarming. The US has a large budget deficit and a rising debt load; there is nothing in the policy environment that suggests either is likely to change soon. The US dollar remains the reserve currency of the world, which allows the debt burden to be sustainable even as it grows. But part and parcel of that sustainability is the idea that policymakers will do what it takes to keep inflation under control and to protect the purchasing power of dollars and the value of Treasury coupon payments. With the executive branch now attacking the Fed’s independence and threatening the production of unbiased economic data, risks to the long-term inflation outlook are rising sharply. Central banks that are not independent have a long and inglorious track record of generating inflation shocks. While we cannot know for sure when that will happen in the US, if the Fed loses its independence, it is all but certain that a shock eventually will occur. For now, markets have made clear that they are focused on the cyclical stability rather than the structural vulnerabilities. That will be true until it isn’t, of course, and when that focus changes it is likely to result in a rapid, and painful, adjustment.

Outlook

  • China’s economy remains largely stagnant, with growth slow and deflation the largest near-term risk. Things didn’t get worse in the third quarter, but neither did they change in any significant way.
  • Policymakers appear unenthusiastic about making major changes, given the uncertain outcome of negotiations between China and the United States around trade and other issues.

Risk Factors

  • Trade negotiations remain a significant source of risk. While a return to prohibitive tariff levels seems unlikely, until a fuller resolution is reached some degree of uncertainty is likely to persist.
  • The property sector remains distressed, impacting both investment and household sentiment. A further deterioration could prove destabilizing.

Overview

China’s economy remains stuck in the mud. With the eventual nature of the relationship between China and the US unclear, uncertainty is higher in China than in other regions. The need to preserve the growth trajectory dictates that production must continue, but with domestic demand weak and access to US markets uncertain, Chinese producers may increasingly be forced to find other markets for their goods. Domestic demand shows little sign of picking up; macroeconomic stimulus has been limited and piecemeal rather than broad and comprehensive, and the result is that households remain cautious. The property sector may be the biggest headwind of all. With housing markets still in a downturn, residential investment activity is going to be limited. At the same time, with many households relying on property markets as a significant portion of their savings and investment, tepid housing markets reduce household wealth and optimism. Until the global situation is more completely sorted out, major stimulus measures appear unlikely, which suggests a few more quarters of muddling along rather than any meaningful change of direction. In the meantime, deflation remains a real risk: with prices in negative territory, a deflationary mindset could set in; if it does that would make a reacceleration even more difficult to achieve.

Outlook

  • Risks to growth remain tilted to the downside but lower uncertainty reduces recessionary risks.
  • From 2026, the outlook for growth should brighten, mostly driven by Germany’s fiscal expansion, which is more front-loaded than initially expected.
  • Inflation has sustainably reached its target as of now. Undershooting risks in the medium term remain and could become more persistent.
  • The European Central Bank (ECB) reached neutral territory but might not be done with easing. We think one more cut this year is warranted but the risks of no cuts are now higher than those of more.

Risk Factors

  • A stronger euro and a possible rerouting of trade flows towards Europe pose a serious medium-term risk to inflation.
  • Geopolitical events continue to represent a major risk. Notably, the inability to end the Russia-Ukraine war lengthens broader risks for the region.
  • Front-loaded spending in Germany represents a clear upside risk to our medium-term outlook. However, the plan could disappoint if not implemented and allocated efficiently, in which case the boost to growth could be delayed.

Overview

While the effective tariff rate is higher than announced last April, diminished tariff-related uncertainty alleviates some of the downside risks to growth. Activity should slow in the second half as export frontloading reverses and domestic growth drivers remain mild. However, risks of a severe recession have clearly lessened. That said, the direct impact of tariffs on growth has yet to fully materialize, which keeps risks to growth skewed to the downside. The medium-term outlook should, however, improve, driven by a more front-loaded German fiscal impulse, and reduce the need for deeper monetary easing. Implementation could be slower than anticipated, but even then, the fiscal shift is consequential for the growth outlook. Consequently, fiscal consolidation in the region will stall given a much larger fiscal deficit than planned by the previous German government. In addition, political instability in France will keep the countries’ deficits at elevated levels.

Inflation is sustainably at target this year, but medium-term disinflationary risks cannot be ignored. While services disinflation has been the slowest, it is nevertheless ongoing and should continue in the coming months. Wage growth is also set to slow markedly by the end of the year, further strengthening the disinflationary process. In addition, a stronger euro and possible rerouting of trade flows to Europe could jeopardize the outlook in the medium term. While the ECB has so far minimized risks of undershoot, even as their forecast incorporates them, we think the picture could change by the end of the year.

Policy rates have now been at a neutral stance since June and considered to be in “a good place” to navigate risks to the economy. The ECB has provided most of the easing already and additional cuts require more convincing evidence. We think risks to growth and inflation continue to be tilted to the downside, but we must acknowledge that the ECB’s current reaction function raises the risk of no further cuts this year. That said, the ECB’s own monetary policy strategy would call for action if larger and more sustained negative deviations of inflation from the target were to materialize in their December forecast.

Overview

Higher inflation and a challenging fiscal outlook are the main focus in the UK. CPI inflation will peak at 4% before gradually easing back to target. Cost-push shocks and higher energy prices are the main drivers; therefore, second-round effects should be contained. Underlying price pressures continued to ease, although momentum has recently slowed, and wage growth should markedly take a step down by the end of the year. Meanwhile, the labor market continues to show signs of weakness. The economy is losing jobs, but at a slower pace than previously suggested. Activity is clearly slowing, which should not help to improve the outlook for the labor market. Even then, the Bank of England’s (BoE’s) reaction function is ultra-sensitive to inflationary risks and the bar for another cut this year is high. We continue to think that a cut in the last quarter is possible, conditional on underlying inflationary pressure easing further. This would ensure that higher headline inflation is not translating into longer-lasting price pressures. The BoE also decided to slow the pace of quantitative tightening to £70 billion over the next 12 months, down from £100 billion. Importantly, as part of the gilt sales that amount to £21 billion for the upcoming year, the BoE decided to sell fewer long maturity gilts (20%) which should help ease tensions on the UK gilt market ahead of the autumn budget. The latter promises to be a headache for the chancellor. Public finances are running above the ORB’s forecast, while the headroom against fiscal targets is likely deep in negative territory. Given little political support for reducing spending, the chancellor will probably implement tax-related measures to restore finances, but such policies could hurt growth in the medium term.

Overview

Political turbulence dominated the Japanese outlook in the third quarter, leaving the economy in something of a holding pattern. With new elections scheduled for early fourth quarter, the scope for economic policy to move back to the fore should increase. An additional rate hike remains possible but far from certain, with much depending on how the new government chooses to conduct fiscal policy, as well as how the new administration’s relationship with the United States develops. In the meantime, domestic demand appears stable, and business sentiment has improved as tariff fears have ebbed. Growth looks set to be stable for the foreseeable future.

Outlook

  • An expanding emerging-markets/developing-markets (EM/DM) growth premium, generally competitively valued EM currencies, and looser global financial conditions might be sufficient to lure capital inflows as the uncertainty about trade tariffs dissipates
  • Target-friendly inflation looks most secure in EM Asia, but inflation is also projected to be within central banks’ tolerance bands in CEEMEA and LatAm in 2026

Risk Factors

  • The deepening of China’s housing sector needs to be monitored closely
  • EM local bond yields and credit spreads are already quite tight

Global economic growth has remained surprisingly resilient year to date, but the gradual infiltration of higher tariffs and trade normalization could still lead to a light stagflationary spell through the turn of the year. This backdrop would ordinarily be challenging for EM. But the Fed’s resumption of its easing cycle and US dollar weakness provide more scope for EM central banks to normalize policy rates to counterbalance the projected soft patch in economic activity.

While the Fed is being swayed by the employment part of their mandate, EM central banks are mostly driven by the favorable inflation outlook. Target-friendly inflation looks most secure in EM Asia, supported by persistent disinflationary impulses in China. But inflation is also projected to be within central banks’ tolerance bands in CEEMEA and LatAm in 2026. To be sure, structural stagflationary risks remain - fueled by higher tariffs and potential shifts in global trade - but China's disinflationary impulses, cyclically contained commodity prices, and stronger EM currencies are expected to dominate the EM inflation trajectory over the next few quarters.

The favorable disinflationary dynamics in China seem to be partly driven by unfavorable property sector dynamics in the country. The deepening of China’s housing sector needs to be monitored closely, but we expect continued targeted policy tweaking to limit spillover to the real side of the economy over the forecast horizon. Our baseline expectation is for EM (excluding China and Russia) to broadly replicate last year’s growth rate in 2025 (c.3.5%) and for economic growth to edge up and further outpace DM in 2026.

An expanding EM/DM growth premium, generally competitively valued EM currencies, and looser global financial conditions might be sufficient to lure capital inflows as the uncertainty about trade tariffs dissipates. While EM currencies have ostensibly benefited from investors increasing hedge ratios on their US dollar holdings, EM portfolio flows also maintained positive momentum during the past two quarters. EM local bond yields and credit spreads are already quite tight, but there is, in our view, scope for selective outperformance if flows remain positive and volatility remains low. We think this could lead to a virtuous cycle where EM currency appreciation fuels yield and credit spread compression.

data.title: Collect 9.9% As Commercial Real Estate Rebounds: Apollo Commercial
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly.

If you have read headlines from many news sources, you'd be led to believe that commercial real estate and commercial real estate lending are in a default apocalypse.

Interestingly, according to the St Louis Fed, the delinquency rate on commercial real estate is still significantly lower than we saw during the Great Financial Crisis or in the early 90s and late 80s.

What we do see, however, is that there is always a trend for higher levels of delinquency and defaults whenever interest rates are higher for longer or the economy is weakening. This should probably be a no-brainer for those who understand that businesses will struggle to pay on their loans if the economy is doing poorly or if the interest rates on floating-rate loans are higher and they're not able to afford them.

For many commercial mortgage real estate investment trusts (REITs), they have been in a period of great difficulty. Financial institutions are typically less willing to be flexible with their lenders to support them in their ability to manage loans that they are struggling to pay on time. Meanwhile, commercial mortgage REITs are more willing to be flexible and offer different solutions or to take over the property entirely and operate it.

We've been highlighting different opportunities in this sector throughout 2025 because we believe that the sector is turning a corner. Many of these commercial REITs are focusing once again on growth instead of playing defense and allowing their portfolios to shrink. It's clear with recent developments that we are not alone in thinking that commercial real estate or even office properties are becoming highly attractive again due to highly discounted prices.

Recently, Rithm Capital (RITM), a REIT that is starting to structure itself more like an asset management company, is taking steps to buy out Paramount Group (PGRE), another REIT that focuses primarily on commercial real estate and specifically office buildings. What this tells us is that there is a growing interest in the extremely discounted valuations of these types of properties, and the sector is heating up again.

Today, I want to look at an opportunity that is growing its portfolio effectively in 2025, and expect that growth to continue going forward.

Let's dive in!

Growing Opportunities In a Challenging Sector

Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance (NYSE:ARI), yielding 9.9%, is a commercial mortgage REIT. ARI originates and holds mortgages that are secured by commercial real estate.

Note: We've covered ARI previously for the Seeking Alpha premium readers. This article should be seen as an update to that coverage and building upon it.

ARI will write loans secured by diverse property types: Source.

Like most commercial real estate lenders, ARI has been negatively impacted by a slow real estate market and rising defaults. High interest rates are a positive for floating rate mortgages, but only if the borrowers are able to pay the higher rates. For the past several years, we've seen a number of defaults in real estate as borrowers went bankrupt or walked away from their properties.

ARI was hit by two particularly large defaults. One, when hospital operator Steward filed for bankruptcy, and two, from 111 West 57th Street, aka "Steinway Tower," a skyscraper with luxury condominiums. ARI realized its losses on Steward, and is in the process of recovering capital from Steinway Tower, collecting funds as units sell: Source.

ARI wasn't hit as hard as some peers, and it is likely that its focus on large-market loans and a significant allocation to Europe, where default rates have been materially lower, has been a positive in the past few years. Today, roughly half of ARI's portfolio is in Europe. Source.

ARI's primary focus is to continue growing its portfolio. Unsurprisingly, the more loans you make, the more interest you collect. A difficult lending market and balance sheet concerns caused ARI to pull in its horns and let the portfolio shrink. That changed in the first half of 2025, with ARI growing its portfolio by over 20%. Source.

This growth is expected to be enough to maintain and expand coverage of its dividend. In the first half, distributable earnings matched the distribution:

A larger portfolio will help increase that cushion and open the door for a distribution raise in 2026. Management projects that if they could free up the capital that is currently tied up in defaulted properties, distributable earnings could be $0.40-$0.60/year higher. That is a target that could reasonably be reached in late 2026 or 2027.

Conclusion

Commercial real estate and office properties are heating up as a sector. We can expect that many beaten-down commercial REITs will enjoy a strong recovery in the next two to three years as the sector turns a corner and the economy recovers from any sort of potential recession that is on the horizon. This means that now, much like RITM, we can be buying into heavily discounted opportunities to enjoy the rebound as it occurs.

As an income investor, I am frequently buying into unloved and beaten-down sectors because they provide high yields and the ability to see capital gains over the long run as the sector recovers. Whenever I buy an investment, I'm looking to hold it for at least two to three years and am willing to hold through dips or downturns in a sector to then enjoy the recovery thereafter. I'm primarily interested in the income that I can receive from my investments, but I'm never going to turn away from a great opportunity to collect capital gains right alongside it. ARI provides new investors the opportunity to do just that and to join alongside myself and other investors who've held for longer to collect wonderful income and see capital gains going forward.

As it stands right now, my initial investment in ARI and the investment of those who bought alongside me in High Dividend Opportunities have provided a positive total return. This means that the total return will only grow with every dividend that we receive and with the recovery of ARI's share price going forward. It's not a tune you don't want to miss.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: I Wouldn't Dare Buy These 3 Popular Investments Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

"There’s more than one way to skin a cat." Or "All roads lead to Rome," to offer a more animal-friendly version of the saying that basically means there’s more than one way to get something done.

In this case, I’m talking about assessing the market.

As almost every one of my more regular readers knows, we have spent a lot of time and energy discussing the market’s valuation in recent years. And don’t worry, this article is different.

If I had to summarize all of my market-focused articles this year, it would be something like this: "Yes, the market is expensive, but under the hood, a lot of opportunities." As generic and useless as it may sound, this stock market truly is a market of stocks.

Let me show you what I mean.

Using Bloomberg data, we see that the S&P 500 is trading a bit above the average analyst target. In fact, it seems it has been "forcing" analysts to bump up their targets since May.

As I promised, I won’t go too much into the details, but this is basically the result of the market trading close to 23.0x earnings. While earnings growth is expected to remain elevated, analysts are very careful when it comes to risking their reputations on S&P 500 calls at these levels. I guess that makes sense, which is why I’m happy it’s not my job.

Ok, that’s the stock market.

Now, onto the market of stocks.

FactSet (FDS), which is a Wall Street data provider, used its numbers to calculate that the S&P 500 is at least 11.4% undervalued over the next 12 months when using a bottom-up approach. In other words, they look at all median price targets of S&P 500 components. Then, based on a company’s weighting in the index, an S&P 500 target price is calculated.

Generally speaking, as we can see below, this price target has been way above the market since at least September 2024. While this is obviously no rocket science, I like this approach, as most investors buy stocks because they are trading below their expected fair value. If enough people do this, the market will benefit. The easiest example of this is NVIDIA (NVDA). While it now benefits from "forced" buying because of its high weighting in the index, it has grown large and boosted the market because of its massive success story.

Needless to say, none of this is super reliable data, as we all know that analysts' price targets are unreliable. That’s not just my opinion or a feeling I have, but also what many researchers have found, including this in-depth study from 2019:

Our results show that some forecasters have done very well, even more so than reflected in earlier studies, but the majority perform at levels not significantly different than chance. - Bailey et al. (2019)

That’s why I believe the quote below perfectly summarizes the current situation in equity land:

“With spreads tight and equity markets pretty stretched on high valuations, it feels like the market is pretty complacent here,” said Stephen Searl, co-head of corporate and municipal teams at Conning. “It’s hard to predict what’s going to flip it into a downturn, but that’s one of our big concerns.” - WSJ

The purpose of this article is to present you with a few companies that I would not buy in this market. This has a number of reasons, one of which is that many readers requested me to cover companies I dislike as well, as I usually only cover the stocks I would buy.

That’s also the reason why these articles usually cover well-known companies. As I wrote in an article titled "3 Super Popular Stocks I Wouldn’t Touch With A 10-Foot Pole," there is no value in discussing companies you wouldn’t buy anyway.

And, as I always need to make clear, I do not hold any short positions, nor do I aim to get anyone to sell their investments. It’s also not my goal to "rage bait" you into flooding the comment section.

No, as always, my only goal is to provide food for thought, as discussing these investments also allows me to cover related "Big Picture" topics that may (read: hopefully will) add value to your research process.

In other words, regardless of my strong language sometimes, this is all about providing info you can use in your research process.

So, let’s start with the first pick, which is an entire sector.

#1 - Don't Buy Utilities (or at Least Be Picky)

The first one is controversial for a number of reasons. Here are two that stand out:

  • I know that many of my readers own utilities.
  • I am bullish on a few of them.

For starters, I don’t hate utilities. However, I don’t like them, either. In fact, despite past investments in companies like Duke Energy (DUK), I never really fell in love with the industry. This has a number of reasons, one of which is that utilities have limited pricing power. For example, most follow a cost-of-service regulation model, which is based on the rate of return. Price hikes need to be roughly in line with expenses to make sure that customers, who are "forced" to buy power and gas from utilities, don’t get price-gouged.

This can be a problem, as utility companies are currently dealing with a huge range of costs, including grid modernization, the ongoing energy transition, and the massive AI revolution.

Although this is currently causing unprecedented growth in total power demand, it doesn’t get me overly excited about utilities. For example, on September 25, The Wall Street Journal wrote, "Higher Electricity Prices Aren’t All Good for Utilities."

The article noted that electricity prices in August were 31% higher compared to four years ago. Overall inflation was up 19% during this period. Ever since the pandemic, power inflation has been an issue, as the chart below shows quite well.

While this shouldn’t be a problem for utilities, you can imagine that this is an issue that gets the attention of politicians, as it’s simply not sustainable for (residential) customers. If there’s one thing I hate (I am fully aware "hate" is a strong word), it’s a situation where politicians are increasingly focused on the companies I own. Trust me, as an investor in defense companies, I know what I’m talking about here.

This is what The Journal wrote in its article:

In New Jersey, the two gubernatorial candidates have made rising electricity bills key talking points of their campaigns. Earlier this year, New York and Rhode Island introduced bills proposing to cap utilities’ allowed return on equity at 4%. By comparison, electric utilities nationwide have historically on average been allowed to earn around 10%, according to data from S&P Global Commodity Insights. - WSJ

According to Lillian Federico, a research director for S&P Global who was interviewed by The WSJ, the pressure on utilities from politicians hasn’t been this high since the 1980s.

This may explain why their returns on equity have taken a big hit relative to the yield on long-term government bonds, as the chart below shows quite clearly.

On top of that, I dislike the average yield. The Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU), which I like to use as a benchmark, yields just 2.7%. That’s a horrible number for income-focused investors.

So, while the XLU ETF continues to make new highs, its relative performance to the S&P 500 remains very weak, as we can see below (XLU/SPY Ratio). Even including dividends, utility investors left a lot of money on the table since 2008.

While I like nuclear-focused utilities in favorable jurisdictions like Vistra Corp. (VST), I prefer midstream companies that come with higher yields, similar safety due to mostly fixed-income contracts in the pipeline industry, and AI benefits from rising natural gas demand and "behind the meter" operations where data centers are being built on top of pipelines in areas like Texas.

In other words, while I am bullish on VST and companies with strong track records like NextEra Energy (NEE), I urge investors to be really careful in this space. As bullish as I am on AI, I think there are better places to invest, especially when it comes to buying income.

#2 - It’s Time to Be Picky in the Mag-7 and Software

I am bullish on various Big Tech companies. This includes Alphabet (GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN). The reasons are quite straightforward. I think Alphabet is one of the best players when it comes to mass adoption of highly successful AI models and the utilization of its massive installed base of users (think of Gmail, YouTube, Google Search, Maps, and more).

Meanwhile, Amazon is using AI and other technologies to rapidly grow its Prime program, expand its moat, and become an even bigger force in e-commerce, cloud computing, and more.

However, in general, I am no fan of simply buying the Mag-7 anymore. As I wrote in articles like "The Paradigm Shift No Investor Can Ignore," I believe we are looking at a prolonged capital rotation.

Because of AI, these companies are increasingly competing with each other. That’s one of the reasons why the four biggest hyperscalers are expected to invest nearly $320 billion in CapEx this year, a number that could reach $410 billion by 2027. Think about that, four companies that spend more than $400 billion in CapEx in a single year…

While they would never admit it publicly, I am convinced they have to spend this amount to avoid being left behind. It’s just too important, as big parts of the future AI market could be "winner takes all."

Bloomberg seems to agree with me, as it noted a general shift away from the Mag-7 among some asset managers:

“Just because the Mag Seven won past tech cycles like mobile, the internet and e-commerce, that doesn’t mean they’ll win here,” said Chris Smith, who oversees $2.4 billion as the portfolio manager of Artisan Partners’ Antero Peak Group. “The next winners will be the ones that address large and unconstrained markets through AI, becoming bigger companies in the future than the Mag Seven are today.”

[...] “A company can become too big to ignore,” said Jurrien Timmer, who helps oversee $16.4 trillion in assets under administration as director of global macro at Fidelity Investments. “It could be that as the AI story evolves new winners take the place of the old winners, even if the previous ones continue to do fine.” - Bloomberg

I’m going a bit off track now, but just think of how much disruption could happen. For example, really good smart glasses could eventually replace smartphones, as we would have endless knowledge in front of our eyes when combined with AI. Also, how important is the personal computer in the future? What about software? With really good AI models, we can maybe write our own software.

Elon Musk, for example, made the case that OpenAI could "eat Microsoft (MSFT) alive." While I am not making the case that this will happen, his comment isn’t far off, as advanced AI models are indeed a huge risk for software.

Essentially, I urge every investor to spend tremendous amounts of time assessing how future-proof their software stocks are. I do not believe the Mag-7 as a group is a great investment for the next decade. I prefer value-focused companies that benefit from AI through adoption and bottleneck opportunities like power and infrastructure.

And software, in general, has become a total minefield if you ask me.

To give you one more example, the chart below shows the energy (XLE) to software (IGV) ratio. A rising ratio means energy stocks outperform. As I believe we will see a rotation to the companies that power AI (and energy in general), I believe this ratio is bottoming and likely to keep rising for at least 1-5 years.

So, don’t panic-sell all of your tech stocks, but please be aware that fundamentals are shifting. The risk/reward is likely much different from what you may think at first sight.

#3 - Beware of the Tide That Lifts All Boats

Last but not least, I want to share a few more thoughts with you on a very specific group of stocks.

I have 2021 vibes.

This is triggered by the massive surge in high-valuation, high-growth stocks. For that, I am using the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) as a proxy, as it owns some of the market’s favorite growth stocks in various niches, for lack of a better description.

In 2020/2021, this ETF ran hot like there was no tomorrow. I remember that people started wearing ARK Invest hoodies and other merch at some point. As it turned out, that marked the peak, as ARKK came down crashing, pressured by rising inflation, higher rates, and the general realization that most hyped growth stocks after the pandemic were trading at astronomical prices.

As most of you may know, many of these stocks lost big time, falling close to (or more than) 90%. Since then, only a handful have meaningfully recovered and made new highs. These companies include Rocket Lab (RKLB) and Robinhood (HOOD), which were two of the "babies" that were thrown out with the bathwater.

Right now, ARKK is up roughly 50% year-to-date, as the market is enjoying strong liquidity and expectations of more rate cuts. This has created the loosest financial conditions since 2022, as the Chicago Fed chart below shows. This is great for growth stocks, as companies with high multiples require a mix of excess capital, falling rates to make discounting future growth more attractive, and favorable investor sentiment.

At this point, I believe investors need to be aware of the risks that come with nearly perfect valuations, as financial conditions can only improve further if the Fed and the Trump administration are successful in their plans to potentially sacrifice the fight against inflation to broaden economic growth.

For example, Rocket Lab is now trading at more than 40x sales. While I am still bullish on it on a long-term basis, I am afraid people expect the massive returns of the past to last. Bear in mind that, based on current sales expectations (FactSet), we would need a 30 price/sales multiple to last to achieve 20% annual returns. At a market cap of $22 billion, which is roughly a quarter of Northrop Grumman’s (NOC) market cap, I don’t see that happening.

Or Robinhood, which now trades at a market cap of more than $100 billion, has a 74x earnings multiple. While EPS growth is expected to accelerate to 27% by 2027, I think the air is getting very thin, as it will eventually see its multiple drop to the 20-30 range the more mature it gets.

As much as I admire Robinhood, investors need to be aware of what we’re dealing with here. If you thought NVIDIA had high expectations, you should check out what goes on in the "ARKK universe." Also, note that HOOD and RKLB are two of the best examples.

There are many with bad financials and even higher future expectations.

My point here is that I want investors who own these kinds of stocks to carefully assess the risk/reward. I’m not saying you should sell them, but give yourself a good reality check.

If the past few months on social media have taught me anything, it’s that these stocks haven’t been this popular since 2021 and that most people are convinced these returns are about to last.

I don’t think they will.

Takeaway

This stock market has turned into a market of stocks.

While I believe its valuation doesn’t prevent it from moving higher, I believe we need to be quite selective in our approach going forward.

That’s why I wrote this article, as being selective includes discussing opportunities that may not provide the best returns going forward.

As bullish as I am on AI-related power demand, I do not believe that utilities, in general, are a great place to be. Yields have come down, CapEx requirements are way up, and political pressure is rising fast. While there are some gems, I think investors need to look twice before making an investment decision in this area.

The same goes for the Mag-7. After more than a decade of explosive growth, they are increasingly competing with each other, forced to spend big time on AI, and likely to lose a few members to this competition. While I like some of them, I urge investors to be careful here as well.

Last but not least, as much fun as the party in ultra-high growth stocks may be, the risk/reward isn’t that favorable anymore. If you own any of these stocks or are planning on buying some, take some time this week and carefully assess the new risk/reward.

Although some may still be great investments, I think the majority of them aren’t. At least not at these prices in this macro environment.

Let me know what you think!

data.title: Grocery Outlet: Why This Bargain Retailer Isn't A Bargain Yet
data.description:
Grocery Outlet Holding Corp. (NASDAQ:GO) got its start back in the 1940s in San Francisco as a simple salvage grocery store.

Over the decades, it grew into a West Coast staple by keeping purchasing and distribution in-house but giving local operators a lot of freedom to run their stores their own way.

Much like Weis Markets (WMK), Village Super Market (VLGEA), and Jerónimo Martins (OTCPK:JRONY), Grocery Outlet was also a family-run business. It stayed under Jim Read and his family’s control for decades—until it eventually got sold off, not to one, but two private equity firms!

The first of these was Berkshire Partners. Yes, you are not mistaken; it was the one who bought Portillo's (PTLO) from Dick Portillo.

Five years after being sold to Hellman & Friedman, Grocery Outlet finally went public.

Initially priced at $15–17 per share, the company saw more interest than it had initially anticipated.

By the end of the day, the stock was priced at $22 per share, raising ~$378 million (a staggering $128 million more than they had initially anticipated!). The shares soon reached the ~$30s, and Hellman & Friedman began reducing its stake.

As time went on, the stock passed its reality check.

When it IPO'd, the stock was trading at around ~40x EV/EBITDA, extremely stretched for a grocery stock.

And precisely this places it in a middle ground: It wasn't nearly as defensive as the big guys like Kroger (KR) or Ahold Delhaize (OTCQX:ADRNY), since it didn't have much cash and carried long-term debt. But at the same time, it wasn't growing fast enough to justify those sky-high multiples either.

Since then, the stock has fallen ~45% from its IPO and trades at an EV/EBITDA of approximately 14x (still a premium compared to other grocers).

One of Eric Lindberg's (the former CEO) last moves was to acquire United Grocery Outlet, adding ~40 stores in the Southeast at a price tag of $62 million.

Grocery Outlet hadn't generated FCF last year; they had to issue $182.4 million in net debt, increasing their long-term debt to $462.5 million (Debt/EBITDA of ~2.2x).

This isn't a huge deal on its own, but when you stack it up against peers with pristine balance sheets, Grocery Outlet does look a bit behind on the capital structure side.

With the CEO change back in February (Jason Potter, who brings more than three decades of grocery and supermarket experience), the focus has shifted. Now it’s all about driving organic growth and easing up on the aggressive store opening pace.

Some Cracks And How The CEO Intends To Fix Them

Because Grocery Outlet relies on opportunistic sourcing, there's a real risk of inconsistent store-to-store experience.

This risk is even greater when you consider that the company's business model prioritizes independent operators.

A rather curious case recently occurred that perfectly illustrates this.

Grocery Outlet just got hit with a class action lawsuit in Oregon. Shoppers say the company was making bogus price comparisons. In other words, they’d advertise that items were cheaper than 'elsewhere,' but when people actually checked, that didn't hold up.

Add to that the inconsistencies with certain brands (which frustrate occasional shoppers) and the stiff competition from giants like Aldi and Lidl, who have ultra-refined supply chains, and you start to see the problem clearly.

With a business model built around bargain shopping, scale doesn't really help. The more stores they open, the less surplus inventory there is to carry for each location.

Is Grocery Outlet’s current model inherently difficult to scale? I believe it is, and with every step they take, they start looking more like a regular grocery chain.

Not that there's anything wrong with that, but they need to tie up some loose ends if they want to keep it that way.

To grow organically and, above all, healthily, Grocery Outlet needs to work on the following fronts:

  • Strengthen Private Labels. This is a movement Grocery Outlet already initiated in FY 2024, but it's still slowly moving forward. They recently took advantage of winery surpluses and launched 'Second Cheapest Wines' for $5 a bottle. However, if I were on this team, I'd focus more on less discretionary foods or beverages, especially fresh produce, to encourage repeatability.

  • Strengthening The Supply Chain. This is one of the new CEO's priorities. The company is currently redesigning its supply chain by transitioning to a single distribution center in the Pacific Northwest, as well as doing the same on the East Coast in Q4 FY 2025. As some products are closer to their expiration dates, agility is crucial here, and this will also allow them to double down on fresh food (as I mentioned earlier) and address the inconsistent experience regarding product availability. I understand that management wants to change the image of a bargain market to something more stable and consistent.
  • Increasing ROI Per location. Grocery Outlet is making its prototype models increasingly cost-effective and scalable. The company currently operates locations between 15,000 and 20,000 square feet but intends to keep them less rigid in some markets, especially now that they have shifted their strategy from penetrating new markets to filling existing ones. The goal is to generate cash returns of ~20% per location.

According to Potter, the steps taken in FY 2025 should give shareholders a higher, more sustainable growth rate; a return to historical levels of compensation (he was probably referring to buybacks); and an adjusted EBITDA margin of around 6%.

A Take On Grocery Stores In California

With 552 stores in Q2 FY 2025 in the United States, Grocery Outlet has almost half of that number in California. After all, they've been there for at least 80 years and have a strong presence in the Sacramento region, the San Francisco Bay Area (where they started), and Los Angeles.

And interestingly, California became the epicenter of the 'Fast Food Exodus,' as price hikes—driven by labor cost offsets under AB 1228—ran well above the national average.

Restaurant traffic took a big hit (especially at QSRs). Some of that traffic shifted to FSRs, where diners felt they were getting better meals at roughly the same price point. But a chunk of it also went straight to supermarkets and grocery stores.

You can see this price difference in the CPI data. While in the last twelve months the price of eating at home increased by 3%, eating out increased by 80 bp more.

Looking at granular geolocation-based traffic data, you can clearly see that grocery stores maintained a gap with QSRs (except for January) and widened it further in August.

Furthermore, while grocery stores only saw a year-over-year traffic decline in February (due to bad weather and wildfires) and July, QSRs only saw traffic growth in three specific months (January, April, and May), renewing the second-worst decline of the year in August (-2.6% year-over-year).

So that traffic gap will probably stick around as long as eating at home stays significantly cheaper for Californians than grabbing fast food.

A quick look at other key markets for Grocery Outlet, such as Washington (~70 stores), Oregon (~60 stores), and Pennsylvania (~30 stores), all saw strong grocery store traffic performance.

Of course, the gap between grocers and QSRs is smaller than that of California, but they still outperform in terms of traffic.

But here’s something interesting: the latest CPI showed the gap between eating at home and eating out narrowed by 30 bps last month. Could that start to hit grocery store traffic?

Maybe—but I think it’s still too early to tell. The gap has been sitting above 100 bps for quite a while, and that’s largely the result of restaurants leaning hard on value promotions.

Low FCF To Consider Top Defensive Pick

Besides not sitting on much cash and having a slightly high debt/EBITDA ratio compared to defensive Top Picks, Grocery Outlet isn't a strong FCF generator.

And that's a bad thing.

When you pick a grocery/supermarket chain stock, you expect consistent returns through dividends and buybacks that somewhat exceed the risk-free rate.

Looking at Grocery Outlet, we can note a few things:

  1. Even with a decentralized management model built around independent operators, Grocery Outlet doesn’t actually run a lean capital structure. Capex eats up most of their operating cash flow—and at times even more than that—pushing free cash flow into the red (like in FY 2024, when FCF came in at -$74.6 million).
  2. OCF is constantly impacted by fluctuations in operating assets. Another factor that inflates it is the SBC, which varied by ~$30 million between FY 2019 and FY 2023. When the SBC was halved, OCF was unable to offset Capex, especially when the latter was especially high, given the pace of openings.

The TTM is a particularly interesting period to observe these fluctuations. Grocery Outlet generated a very strong OCF of $195.1 million (one of the highest in the last decade, second only to FY 2023), but it was artificially inflated by changes in operating assets of $54.8 million and completely eclipsed by Capex of $221.1 million.

Even if Grocery Outlet returns to generating FCF of ~$51.2 million (normalizing by considering the FY 2020–2023 average), we would be talking about an FCF yield of ~1.5%, well below the average for grocery/supermarket stocks.

But as I said before, Grocery Outlet has changed course, and we'll most likely see positive FCF again in FY 2026.

But by how much?

We know they're targeting between 33 and 35 stores for FY 2025 as a whole (opening 9 net locations in Q2 FY 2025 alone), which is still a strong pace. Therefore, for FY 2025, Capex should still remain at historically high levels.

Let's look at some scenarios:

  1. Capex returns to ~$125 million, in line with the FY 2020–2022 period. Assuming revenue of $4.98 billion (in line with Wall St estimates), an EBITDA margin of ~5–6%, and an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate of 85%, we would arrive at an OCF of approximately $232.9 million. In this case, FCF would be almost $108 million. Here, we would see an FCF yield of ~3.3%, approaching the industry median.
  2. If Capex remains high (above $200 million) and OCF assumptions remain the same, FCF would be just over $30 million, compressing beyond the pre-pandemic period.
  3. If they choose to keep it at ~$160 million, FCF would come in at ~$73 million (FCF yield below industry median again).

With this FCF, I see them reducing long-term debt and slightly offsetting the effects of SBC (as they already do through the latest $100 million buyback agreement).

If you pay attention, even though they spent approximately $50 million in the TTM on buybacks, this doesn't move the needle as much as it seems when the story is shareholder returns.

Shares outstanding dropped only about 1% year over year. In other words, as long as they keep leaning on SBC, returns are going to stay stuck in the low single digits annually.

But what if Grocery Outlet starts paying dividends in FY 2026? Could they afford it?

To analyze this, we need to arrive at a decent figure. I'd say that, to start, a payout of ~40% for a defensive stock is quite interesting. Based on EPS of $0.91 for FY 2026, dividends per share would be approximately $0.36, an allocation of $35.3 million.

This is payable in two of the three scenarios I modeled.

In any case, I wouldn't envision annual returns coming from a low-single-digit shareholder yield (likely ~1% in FY 2025 and rising to ~2.5% from FY 2026 and beyond).

Is Grocery Outlet Cheap?

Before we discuss the valuation itself, I'd like to give you some color on management's full-year guidance and its modifications in Q2:

  • Same-store sales growth between 1—2%;
  • Addition of 33 to 35 net new stores;
  • Gross margins of 30—30.5% (gross profit of approximately $1.41 billion for the full year based on Wall St. revenue estimates);
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $260—270 million;
  • Adjusted EPS of $0.75—0.80.

For EBITDA, I will be considering ~$240 million (based on a 5% EBITDA margin and Wall St. consensus revenue), slightly below adjusted EBITDA.

As a reference group for my Comps Model, I selected a blended group of off-price/closeout peers, including companies like Ollie's Bargain Outlet (OLLI), Sprouts Farmers (SFM), Dollar Tree (DLTR), and Dollar General (DG). The 5-year average of these companies' EV/EBITDA multiples gave me a multiple of ~15x.

In the short to medium term, I landed on a price target of $18.60 for Grocery Outlet.

I see that as fair, since the stock deserves a meaningful discount to Ollie's Bargain Outlet (its closest comp) given Ollie's is growing faster and more cleanly, with zero long-term debt and over $300 million in cash and equivalents.

Looking out to FY 2026, if we assume EBITDA of around $300 million (with margins ticking up 100 bps to 6% and some deleveraging), that would point to a price target—discounted back to today—of about $24.

In this sense, I see an upside of ~14.8% based on the current scenario and 48% based on a potential re-rating of balance sheet restructuring and normalized FCF.

The problem is that until then, you won't see significant returns from dividends and buybacks, which is always a bad thing when looking at these types of stocks.

I’m viewing Grocery Outlet as more of a defensive play among food retailers, so for now I’m calling it a ‘Hold.’ That said, if you’re looking for a turnaround or re-rating story, this could be an interesting play, but I would like a bigger discount.

But how much discount would be enough?

In the short term, I’d peg fair value around $18. That’s where the stock landed after a strong Q2, and to me, it’s already starting to trade at a slight discount.

So if the stock slides into the low $10s like it was before the rally, I would see a buy trigger.

data.title: AI Monthly: Rising Usage, Uneven Gains
data.description:
By Amrita Naik Nimbalkar and Diederik Stadig

10% of the world’s population now uses ChatGPT

A recent publication by OpenAI reported that the number of users ChatGPT sees each week is now around 700 million, which translates to roughly 10% of the world population. With a deployment that vast, GenAI is not only being increasingly adopted in enterprises but is also becoming a part of our everyday lives. In June 2024, 53% of the messages were non-work-related, which in June 2025 stands at 73%, leaving 27% categorised as work-related messages.

77% of the conversations are reported being used for practical guidance (29%), information seeking (24%), and writing (24%). However, when it comes to only work-related messages, it is largely used for writing (40%), practical guidance (24%) and technical help (10%).

AI-driven productivity gains vary depending on the nature of the task

Based on the output that the user wants to receive, the messages sent to ChatGPT can also be classified into three types: doing, asking and expressing. These have different economic implications. Doing messages – using ChatGPT to perform tasks such as writing, generating code, analysing data, etc. – produce directly usable output and can be seen as automation of tasks. Asking messages – seeking information to be better informed – help in supporting user decisions but do not directly produce output, serving as an augmentation tool. Expressing messages, which are neither for information seeking nor performing a task, contribute negligibly to productive output.

Currently, 35% of messages are doing related and 52% are asking, highlighting the dual role of ChatGPT as a co-worker producing output and a co-pilot aiding problem-solving. The most common tasks (shown in the figure below) can be combined into two main functions; a) gathering, documenting, analysing, and interpreting information, and b) thinking, solving problems and providing consultation. This shows that generative AI currently aids people with either the quality or speed of their work, which is a productivity improvement.

Share of work-related messages sent to ChatGPT

Usage patterns vary widely across demographic groups

Around its release, approximately 80% of active users of ChatGPT were men. Currently, usage is roughly balanced between men and women but there are differences in how they use it. Women tend to send mostly writing and practical guidance queries, while men tend to use it more for technical help, information seeking, and multimedia. The share of work-related messages also increases with age, education level, and for highly paid professional occupations. This suggests that GenAI might currently be providing more productivity benefits for users who already have access to better jobs and education, validating earlier concerns about its disproportionate impact. A recent Stanford study in the US further supports this, by showing a decline in employment for early-career employees.

Benefits of AI could be distributed unevenly

The disparities in usage might suggest unequal benefits. Men tend to use GenAI at work for tasks centred around asking - such as information seeking - reflecting a more augmented, decision-supported role for AI. In contrast, women more often use AI for doing tasks like writing, which are more closely linked to automation and have a higher risk of replacement. Moreover, highly educated employed users stand to gain more, while opportunities for junior employees could be limited.

This makes us think about something Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang’s said recently: “You’re not going to lose your job to AI, you’re going to lose your job to someone who uses AI.” While embracing this technology offers clear productivity gains, those who use it best will have a distinct advantage.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
