data.title: Dividend Cut Alert: Big Yields That Are Likely About To Get Slashed
data.description:
Often, when high-yield stocks plunge, it gets my attention because I like to invest in high-yield stocks that are out of favor, thereby being able to load up on investments that deliver very juicy passive income to my portfolio. Then, once they go back into favor, I can trim or even sell the positions and recycle the capital into other opportunities that have gone out of favor, thereby rinsing and repeating and accelerating my compounding process as well as my dividend growth. However, there are also times when stocks sell off for good reason. In this article, I am going to share two double-digit dividend yields that I think are about to cut their dividends and are also facing some underlying fundamental challenges.

Big Dividend Cut Warning #1

The first I am going to discuss is FS KKR Capital (FSK). FSK recently reported net investment income of $0.62 per share and adjusted net investment income of $0.60 per share for the quarter. Both of these figures were problematic because they failed to cover even the base dividend of $0.64, let alone the total dividend of $0.70 per share that included the supplemental distribution. Additionally, NAV per share plunged from $23.37 to $21.93 over the course of just one quarter. This reflects an underlying portfolio that is shedding value quite rapidly and is not even covering its full dividend.

To make matters worse, the leverage ratio has now ballooned to 1.2x, which is near the upper end of its target range, meaning it has limited ability to add incremental leverage to support the dividend. Meanwhile, the company’s non-accrual rate at 3% of fair value—while not alarmingly high—is still above stronger peers like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL).

On top of this, FSK has a material amount of debt maturing at the beginning of 2026—just three and a half months away—that currently carries a 3.4% interest rate. When refinanced, the company will almost certainly have to pay several hundred basis points higher, further straining dividend coverage. Adding to these problems, payment-in-kind income is fairly high at 13.3% of total investment income, which raises further concerns about the quality and sustainability of earnings.

When you combine all of these issues—rising leverage, elevated PIK income, above-average non-accruals, and looming refinancing at much higher interest rates—FSK looks like a near lock to cut its dividend, and not just modestly, but quite substantially. Additionally, the weak underlying fundamentals of the business point to further potential downside. As a result, I am steering clear of FSK right now.

Big Dividend Cut Warning #2

The other double-digit yield that I think is about to cut its dividend—and that I am steering clear of right now—is CION Investments (CION). Like FSK, CION is a business development company that has pulled back sharply recently, pushing its yield into very attractive territory. However, while the yield of 15.7% looks highly compelling alongside FSK’s 18.75% dividend yield, CION is also struggling to sustain its payout.

Its net investment income per share for the most recent quarter was just $0.32, while its non-accruals on an amortized cost basis stood at 3.03%. Meanwhile, it paid out a distribution of $0.36 per share in the quarter, which shows that its net investment income is not covering the dividend. In addition, its debt-to-equity ratio has soared to 1.39x, above its upper target limit, leaving no room to add incremental leverage to support the dividend. In fact, it will likely have to work to bring leverage down instead, which makes a dividend cut all the more likely in the near future.

Adding to the pressure, CION has fixed-rate debt maturing in February of next year at a 4.5% interest rate, which will almost certainly need to be refinanced at a materially higher rate. This refinancing will further weigh on its already weak distribution coverage. Beyond that, its NAV per share has eroded considerably over time, and the company generates a high proportion of its earnings from payment-in-kind loans—17% of total income—similar to FSK. These PIK loans are riskier, and the company’s long-term NAV erosion confirms the underlying weakness.

Given these negative factors and growing risks of a dividend cut, we are steering clear of CION as well, despite its substantial discount to NAV and elevated yield.

Investor Takeaway

While we do see opportunities in the BDC (BIZD) space right now, we prefer higher-quality names with strong fundamentals, minimal PIK income, low non-accruals, and little refinancing risk. CION and FSK fail on all of these counts and are already falling short of covering their dividends with net investment income. Therefore, we expect substantial dividend cuts ahead, as well as potential further downside in NAV per share, especially if the economy weakens from here. For these reasons, we are avoiding both in favor of higher quality BDC opportunities in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: VTEL: I Fear It's The Wrong Strategy For The Muni Market
data.description:
We Have to Look at Those Internal Workings

One way to work out how a machine works is just to turn it on. The problem with this is that it will then start doing whatever, and we might not actually want whatever. The other way is actually to examine the machine and then work out how the internals interact. That way we can have an idea of what the whatever is going to be and decide whether we want to flip the switch before we get that whatever.

Or, in a less tortured style, we should be aware of what we're buying before we buy it. This, sadly, means having a look at certain details and background.

Vanguard Long-Term Tax-Exempt Bond ETF (BATS:VTEL)

This is a new fund, launched back in May. It's thus not possible to give a detailed analysis of past events. But - see above about the internal workings - we can have a look at the moving parts inside it and then at least suggest what the likely output is going to be.

This is an exchange-traded fund, which brings with it certain attributes. When we buy, we're adding to the fund; when we sell, taking from it. This means if there's a lot of selling, the fund might blow through the cash margin and have to liquidate positions to pay out. If really a lot try to sell, then this can lead to dumping of positions, which damages the asset value of the fund - thus spooking yet more selling, and we enter a redemption cascade. This is akin to - almost exactly the same as, really - a bank run, and no one wants to be last to collect their money in one of those. Which is why cascades are dangerous - but whether it can happen depends upon the liquidity in the underlying investment. Here the fund's in municipal bonds - we're liquid enough not to have to worry.

As an ETF, an open ended fund, there's no leverage here, and it's always going to trade within tenths of a percentage point of net asset value. We're also never going to have any of that nonsense about return of capital to boost yields or anything.

The basic structure of the fund is to track an index and, in doing so, be long interest rate risk and minimise credit risk. My own opinion - please note, opinion, not proof - is that the muni market is better played the other way around, but to illustrate that, we need to go through the details of bonds and the bond market.

Bond Markets

Treasuries are lending to the Federal Government. Yes, I know, we often think of it the other way around - other people issue bonds. But they're just the piece of paper that can be traded and that tracks our loan to whoever it is. Buying a bond is lending to someone. The Feds haven't gone bust this past couple of centuries, so we assume - in fact, state - that Treasuries are risk-free. This isn't, not wholly and absolutely, true. Other governments have gone bust in recent years. But we do still make the assumption, right across financial markets, that Treasuries are "risk free".

But that's credit risk - there's still interest rate risk here. This is the idea that when interest rates change, so does the value of every bond already in issue. If rates were 3% yesterday and 4% today, then a bond issued yesterday pays $3 per year on $100 and one of today pays $4. These two bonds have a different capital value - whatever the net present value of $1 on $100 times however many years the bonds run for.

We can see that longer bonds (what we call long "tenor" bonds) will change more in price than shorter - a 30-year changes more than a 30-day.

There is no real way around interest rate risk. Which makes us all sad because it's also true that interest rates rise the longer the tenor of the bond (well, unless the yield curve is inverted, and that's a newsworthy event). So, we have to accept more interest rate risk in order to get that higher interest rate. Which does make sense, as we get paid to take risks in financial markets - and the one great trick is never to take a risk you're not being paid for.

Corporates are lending to companies, and we know those can go bust. This introduces credit risk into the idea and also means corporates pay higher interest rates or coupons than Treasuries.

Now there is a way to deal with - not eliminate, but certainly reduce - credit risk, which is diversification. We own small amounts of many things, and if one goes bust, that's an annoyance, not a disaster. That, in bond markets, brings its own little problem.

We can buy new at issuance in $1,000 lots for near all bond issues and get paid back at redemption. But the secondary markets for bonds can be more difficult. The European corporate bond markets work on a $200k par value minimum lot, for example. Which is too large for the individual investor to be able to be diversified, obviously. So, the answer becomes to be in a fund. The fund is large enough to be diversified; we own a fraction of the fund, and we achieve the goal that way.

The third type of bonds - by this classification—is municipal. Here we've both interest rate and credit risk, so they should pay more than Treasuries. They don't. Now, true, the credit risk is pretty small - the usual estimate is the default rate is 0.1%. But even that does mean they should pay more than Treasuries, even if less than corporates. But as I say, they don't; they pay less than Treasuries.

This is because the interest received is Federal income tax-free (and usually, for an in-state bond, free of in-state income tax for in-state residents). That tax-free part produces a weight of money interested in buying the bonds; the weight of money itself reduces the coupon/interest rate that has to be paid on the municipal bonds.

Tax Equivalent Yields

This then produces a little valuation problem for us. We can't directly compare the yields on Treasuries and corporates with municipals given that one is tax-free and the others aren't. Worse, because different people pay different marginal (that is, on the next $ of income) tax rates, we cannot even have one calculation for everyone.

What we do is tax equivalent yields. What would someone at this tax rate have to be paid by a taxable investment in order to get the same yield as this non-taxable one? Not easy; fortunately, people publish tables for us:

There is no hard and fast rule here - how could there be? Different people get different levels of angry at having to pay tax. But the rule of thumb is that below a 24% tax rate, you're probably better off earning taxable income and paying the tax - that weight of money that pushes down the tax-free yields - and above that, you should at least think seriously about muni bonds.

VTEL Details

Now that we've armed ourselves with the background, we can have a look at the working parts within VTEL.

The full list can be seen there. But this is the important thing about the holdings:

Number of bonds 1741

Ah. That means they're not being selective. Well, they are a bit; the total munis universe is tens of thousands. But they're tracking an index, and they're doing that by holding a very wide range. Which is what they say they're doing, which is nice, our own analytical abilities and the fund agreeing:

To track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment-grade segment of the U.S. municipal bond market with remaining maturities greater than 10 years. The fund employs an indexing investment approach designed to track the S&P 10+ Year National AMT-Free Municipal Bond Index using a sampling technique to closely match key benchmark characteristics.

They're sampling, not selecting.

OK, but there are two more things about the holdings we want:

And:

Ah, now I have certain prejudices - you know, those things more unreasonable than opinions but more strongly held? - about the muni market. And I think that this strategy is going to lead to not particularly great results. Shadowing an index by sampling, long tenor, and high credit quality - I think all three of those can be argued against.

Please do note, again, this is opinion. I am not saying Vanguard is wrong; I just think it's possible to do things a different way to better effect.

Costs and Returns

The costs here are great - 0.09%. But we do have to remember that costs aren't wholly the thing. It's returns after costs that are. High fees for outperformance are fine, great even; low fees for underperformance aren't worth paying.

Median bid/ask is 0.11%, so this isn't something to be trading in and out of on a daily basis - this is a hold or nothing.

We can't look at the Seeking Alpha number, a 1% distribution, because that's totting up the past 12 months, and the ETF hasn't been running that long. So:

30 day SEC yield 4.15%

And, well, yes. So there's very little credit risk, and that's fine when we think about the tax e yield as above.

There's also a little technical point here. As the fund is so new, it's not got any holdover bonds it bought when interest rates were either lower or higher. So this isn't a yield I'd expect to last through the interest rate cycle. Everyone else has bonds they bought when the Fed Funds rate was on the floor three and five years back and so on; this fund hasn't. Precisely because the fund is new and only holds bonds bought when interest rates were at—or about—current levels, this yield might flatter.

Why I'm Wrong

There certainly will be those who simply buy a Vanguard muni bond ETF because, you know, it's Vanguard. Lovely low fees; it's very widely diversified to shadow that index. Tuck some of this away for the decades. For those who do think that way - it helps them sleep at night and so on - then fine. My quibbles would therefore be wrong.

There is one other point. Precisely because the holdings were all bought in these past few months, if interest rates fall - that interest rate risk working in our favour - then this fund will do very well. We might even look at the employment and so on numbers coming out - or think that the short-term effect of all these tariff changes is going to be negative, whatever the long - and predict lower interest rates to counter a slowdown. At which point this fund really will do well in capital terms.

I'm not sure that a muni bond ETF is quite where we should speculate on the general level of interest rates, but it's worth mentioning.

My Opinion

VTEL is, in my opinion, going to be an entirely sound muni bond ETF. However, I think it's going to be possible to do better elsewhere. That combination of shadowing an index, long tenor bonds and low credit risk strikes me as not the finest strategy.

Index funds in munis, I have noted (here with SPDR Nuveen ICE Municipal Bond ETF (TFI)), seem to do less well than more selective funds. Of course, it's always possible that I'm not seeing the funds that have tried to be selective and have not done well. Long tenor (here, Nuveen Municipal Value Fund (NUV)) seems to involve what can be significant capital risk. Without boosting the distribution sufficiently to make up for it. And a great deal more controversially, I think that credit risk is overworried about. If the overall default rate is 0.1%, then sticking with investment grade only seems to me to be missing a trick. I explained it more (with iShares Short Duration High Yield Muni Active ETF (SHYM)) here:

Now "not rated" in the bond markets usually means something like "complete dog and not touching it with your bargepole let alone mine". CCC isn't much better. Actually, in large parts of the bond market, BBB isn't thought of as much better than that.

At which point we could just assume that SHYM is achieving high yield by taking on lots of credit risk—the standard complaint is that we can only get paid in financial markets by taking risk.

However, the actual default rate in the muni markets is really very low—that 0.1% estimate above. So we could be dealing with comparative credit worthiness, not actual. Sure, a BBB muni is less creditworthy than an AAA but if the overall default rate is tiny, how much difference does that actually make?

Also, not rating means that the issuer hasn't paid a rating agency to rate the bond. This is not uncommon for small issues either. Fund managers can still decide upon their own opinion by having their own look at the finances of the issuer. That is, "unrated" does not mean "not worthy of a rating" it means "no one has paid for a rating" which is a very different thing.

Effectively, a fund heavily into unrated bonds is using the calculations of its own managers rather than the calculations of the rating agencies. Many funds won't touch unrated bonds, so there's a possibility that they're underpriced. Maybe, possibly, we cannot be categorical about this.

That, I think, is the special secret sauce here. Grasping that unrated in the muni market does not mean high credit risk, even if unrated (and other low-rating bonds) pay higher coupons. Thus, without a (substantial that is) increase in risk, it is possible to gain a higher income. Which is the great trick in any financial market—to get paid for taking risks you're not actually taking or to be overpaid for the ones you are.

I approve of this—and again, it's not a trick; it's an insight—and strategy.

Now, note again, that's an opinion, possibly a prejudice. But I've not seen evidence against it as yet.

I'm sure that VTEL will be a just fine exemplar of the strategy being followed - index shadowing high credit rating long tenor munis. I just don't think that's the correct strategy to be following in munis. My opinion is that selective, low credit rating (but checked by the fund manager directly) and either short or long tenor work better. After all, SHYM does pay a larger distribution even while having substantially lower interest rate risk.

So, VTEL isn't for me.

data.title: Opendoor Technologies: House Flipping Wrapped In Tech Marketing
data.description:
I am very pessimistic on Opendoor Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:OPEN), and I do not buy the hype. This article is completely my opinion and analysis. The company likes to present themselves as a tech revolution, but in reality, I see a house-flipping model, which has been tested before and was proven ending up in big losses.

Financial Health And Results

From the first look, Opendoor in 2025 looks good—the losses are easing, and revenue is step by step recovering. But if we look deeper, this is not yet a breakthrough. The numbers are getting better, but I see some structural problems behind them.

Firstly, profitability. I know many wrote about this, but let's still look into this. In 12 quarters, Opendoor showed positive net income once. In Q2 2025, -$29 million was presented as progress because it was lower than the last quarter's hundreds of millions in losses. The EBITDA part looks bad as well: even in the best quarters, it is balancing around zero with episodic positive/negative results. In other words, the business is not capable of generating money until we take into account interest rates or costs.

Secondly, the capital is still expensive. Opendoor is paying around $30-40 million in interest rates every quarter. These costs eat any potential margin and direct money to creditors, not shareholders. Yes, we saw the Fed easing the interest rates, and it does give some breathing room, but not enough to bring value to shareholders.

Liquidity is an issue as well. The business model is based on real estate buying; Opendoor always has to have, let's call it, “ammunition” for transactions—big cash reserves. When the market is lagging, the only way to maintain activities is to get into debt or issue new shares. Which they have been doing, and in 3 years, the share count rose from 634 million to 729 million; this is around 15% of losses to existing shareholders. The operational cash flows are fluctuating from positive to negative; this shows that the company cannot sustain itself through operations and depends on capital injections, which does increase the risk of dilution again.

Cyclicality. Revenue is dependent on real estate market activities. It’s going to take one interest rate jump, and the demand for real estate will go down, and in one quarter, Opendoor can burn a few hundred million. We saw that in 2022-2023, when the market broke and put the company into the deep red.

Currently, I believe this business is built on hype; the investors are betting that the real estate market is going to recover and grow fast. But if that does not happen, investors will see that the company is walking on razor blades.

Personally, when I see the company, which in 12 quarters did not show profitability (only one quarter in Q2 2023), every quarter is paying millions in interest rates, and the only innovation is an algorithm, this does not remind me of a technology company, but rather an experiment, which most of the time ends up not in a revolution, but liquidation.

Opendoor EBITDA (mln. USD)

Quarter GAAP Net Income Adjusted EBITDA
Q2 2025 –29 +23
Q1 2025 –92 –31
Q4 2024 –106 –45
Q3 2024 –110 –64
Q2 2024 –101 –55

We see adjusted EBITDA being positive for the first time, but this does not mean that the company became profitable. This is non-GAAP, and that means it was calculated taking out amortization, restructuring costs, and so on. It's good that they showed first positives, but the trend remains, and investors should understand that this might be just a temporary effect, not a breakthrough in business.

Why Am I So Bearish?

Q2 2025 Results show that 36% of inventory was in the market longer than 120 days, when a year ago this number was at 14%. This means that more than a third of the real estate they bought is staying unsold for more than four months—and my experience (when I was doing that) says that this tempo in real estate is a sign that pricing is not working. So, if algorithms were really advantageous, the houses would sell faster, but currently, most likely, they will have to give discounts or lower the price to sell.

Another indicator is that their contribution margin in Q2 2025 was around 4.4%, when in 2024 it was 6.3%. This shows that even sold real estate generates lower profitability, and algorithms cannot sustain stable unit economics. When margins are going down and the inventory cycle is getting longer, the company is losing its flexibility and becomes dependent on capital injections.

In the long term, this becomes an even sadder problem. The transaction volume in Q2 2025 went down to 1757; this is down 63% from last year. I believe, and this is my opinion, and I am bearish, the company does not believe in its model, and this is the sign that they are not stabilizing, but the risk is being managed as the algorithm seems to be wrong too many times and does not give stable results.

This also shows that, fundamentally, iBuying models have weaknesses. Technology cannot change real estate market cyclicality. Housing demand is dependent on interest rates, customer sentiments, and the local market.

Business Model Risks

I have a strong opinion on TSLA stock: that it is not a technology stock, and neither, I believe, is Opendoor. The company likes to present themselves as revolutionizing the real estate market. Mass media portrays that they are the next Amazon or the next Airbnb. But the reality is much simpler: they are buying houses, then trying to sell them for bigger prices. I used to do this before 2022 myself and can tell you that it's not really that easy. The difference is that they are taking the broker out of the deal and have an algorithm that makes a decision about what price to offer.

Every transaction is one-time, capital intensive, and risky. Every time when Opendoor buys real estate, it takes a pricing risk. If the market is going down, they are left with the asset, whose value is going down. And it is not a stock; it's not liquid enough to sell fast and cut off the losses. From my point of view, this is simple “house flipping,” just in a more beautiful box.

Where do I see the main problem? That the real estate market is unpredictable. Even the biggest data giants could not predict and model the prices. There are hundreds of factors, from neighborhood reputation to interest rate changes. Zillow tried the same model but had to go with really big losses.

Yes, I know that many will say Opendoor is the one main player left in the market. But is this an advantage? I think not. This is the last company in the risky business model, which was tried by others and got buried. I truly think this is not a tech disruptor but a simple digital house flipper.

Let’s Go Through Some Examples

Zillow Offers—most likely one of the most infamous iBuying examples. In 2018, Zillow had the biggest flow of consumers and access to market data and decided to become a buyer and seller themselves. Algorithms had to make sure the pricing was effective. But reality was different: algorithms were almost always wrong, the company bought more expansive real estate, and when the market in 2021 became unstable, the losses were up to nearly $900 million. Result: the program failed, hundreds were fired, and Zillow turned back to the traditional low-risk model.

RedfinNow—another lesson. Redfin tried the “iBuyer” program as an addition to their brokerage activities. In 2022, they had to admit that the model was not working, margins were low, there was a big capital risk, and the market was unstable, which generated millions in losses. Result: the company closed the program and wrote off asset values.

WeWork—even though it was in another sector, the story is similar. Investors were buying the “tech startup” vision, but in reality, it was traditional, capital-intensive office subrenting. When the technology label fell off, it became just a simple rent middleman with big losses. Result: eventually filed for bankruptcy.

All these examples just show a trend: technology does not make capital-intensive, low-margin businesses profitable. It can be house flipping, it can be office sub-renting—the algorithms and apps do not change simple economy logic.

Competition Advantage

Opendoor today is presented as the “last big iBuyer player.” But this is not an advantage but a warning. If all the competitors run off from the model, it will likely be because it does not work; no one runs from money-making machines. But it is my opinion, and I am bearish on the stock, and I am biased, so I don’t believe in the Opendoor revolution, but the late stage of the same, already tested and unsuccessful model.

When investors talk about “moat,” that defense for a business against competition, most of the time it means patents, platform network effect, or a strong ecosystem. Sadly, Opendoor does not have it. This is not Amazon, which created a supply chain that is almost impossible to clone, or Airbnb, which has millions of unique consumers.

The technology side is not giving an advantage as well. Their algorithms for real estate prices are not a miracle—almost every bank, hipoteca fund, or an investment REIT has access to big databases and analytical models. Even if Opendoor presents a “unique pricing engine,” in reality, algorithms have a tendency to be wrong (we saw that in the Zillow example). In other words, any player with big capital can clone their idea.

I can provide some examples to prove my point. Giants like Blackstone or Invitation Home are managing tens of billions of dollars in real estate portfolios. They have cheap access to capital, long-term experience, and scale effects, which lets them buy real estate much more effectively than Opendoor. Even traditional banks, in theory, could get into this market—their financing rates are a few times lower. If players like these decided to copy the model, Opendoor would not have any way to stop them.

And this is the main problem I see from the competition side. There is no lock-in effect. Clients do not feel any loyalty and can sell their house through Opendoor today or tomorrow through a traditional broker or another iBuyer. There is no ecosystem to hold them as customers. I believe that in the long term, Opendoor does not have sustainable competitive barriers. They have an operational mode, which depends on cheaper capital and short-term market dynamics.

Positives

Even though my position is very bearish, I have to admit there are some bull case scenarios as well.

Management is actively speaking about their new AI tool that would let them price real estate better and lower the risks of transactions. In theory, better prognosing could indeed shorten inventory holding time and make the contribution margin better. If algorithms become more effective, I believe it would be a major structural change.

The company is trying to manage its risk by going to "agent-assisted" and "platform" activities. This is good because it means the company could not only depend on asset buying and selling but also on being intermediaries as well. This could ease on their capital-heavy business, and margins could become stronger.

Another very positive thing, as per all real estate businesses, is that the FED is lowering interest rates. And lower interest rates could make financing cheaper, which would make rotating assets faster, as the main logic of this business is to have better transaction volume and speed.

All the factors are truly able to make some tailwinds in the future. But I believe they are not eliminating the fundamental risks.

Summary

At first look, Opendoor numbers look great; the losses are lower, and revenue is somewhat rising. But looking deep enough, I don't see any breakthrough. The company is not showing any profits for 12 quarters except the one in 2023, and interest rates eat any margins that could show up.

The most dangerous currently are the operational facts. Inventory is not being sold. Acquisition volume fell 63% in a year. This all shows that the "unique" advantage is not giving results. And all this reminds me of Zillow and Redfin stories - both tried the iBuying model, and both had to get out with big losses.

I believe real estate logic will not change; the company is a capital-heavy real estate flipper. That is why I am not buying the hype and rating the stock Strong Sell.

data.title: SCHD: If You're Considering Selling, Buy QDVO Instead
data.description:
Introduction

Schwab's U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) is very popular with investors, and for good reason. The fund offers passive income to investors, a low-cost structure, and an attractive valuation, making them perfect for retirees or those seeking reliable income.

However, SCHD has underperformed over the past year, in the red at the time of writing. While I think SCHD is still a great ETF to own, I have to admit I've been thinking recently of selling them for another ETF I've been doing a lot of research on.

While I have no specific timeline of when I'm planning to sell if I decide, a decision could be made in the near future. So, if you hold SCHD and are disappointed in its underwhelming performance, this article suggests an alternative ETF you may find as a suitable replacement.

In this article, I discuss SCHD's performance, strategy, and a compelling case as to why this unique ETF may be a suitable replacement.

Previous SCHD Buy Case

I covered SCHD back in August in an article titled: Wouldn't Call It Dead Money.

Although the ETF had underperformed, its high-quality holdings, income reliability, and low expense ratio made it a viable choice inside an income-focused portfolio.

SCHD was also designed to provide downside protection for investors, as its sector allocation favored stability over growth. But as a result, the ETF's share price lagged more growth-focused competitors.

Despite the likelihood of seeing near-term underperformance, I still believed SCHD offered long-term value and income stability for a lower interest rate environment.

A Look At SCHD

Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF is a favorite amongst income investors for the reasons I state below:

  • Cost-Efficiency (expense ratio 0.06%)
  • Qualified dividends
  • Income reliability & dividend growth
  • Defensive portfolio consisting of quality dividend holdings
  • Track record

With an inception date of 10/20/2011, SCHD has performed well overall. In short, the ETF has done its job, providing solid returns over the past 14 years. In October of last year, a lot of Schwab's (SCHW) funds conducted 3-for-1 stock splits.

Since their inception, SCHD has underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) ETFs: Vanguard's (VOO) and SPDR's (SPY) in price returns.

But they have managed to beat iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV), up nearly 214% compared to roughly 129% for the latter.

In total returns, they've managed to outperform HDV as well as Vanguard's High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM) by less than 1%.

Before the fund split its shares last year, they showed solid dividend growth, growing the dividend from $0.1217 to $0.7545. Since the split, SCHD's dividends have averaged $0.2585 a share.

Their latest distribution was a slight increase from last quarter's $0.2602 to $0.2604. Nothing special, but again, SCHD does its job. And going forward this is likely to continue as a result of their portfolio.

Currently, SCHD's portfolio is mostly concentrated in the Energy (XLE) sector, with this making up 19.23%. Technology (XLK) makes up the 5th largest sector, part of the reason the ETF has underperformed over the past year.

But the defensive nature of SCHD's portfolio is what makes them unique and a reliable income payer. Respectable companies like AbbVie (ABBV), Chevron (CVX), Home Depot (HD), Verizon (VZ), Altria (MO), and The Coca-Cola Company (KO) make up some of the top holdings.

Moreover, all these companies have strong track records, pay reliable dividends, and are likely to continue to perform even in an economic downturn should one come to fruition.

So, if SCHD is so wonderful, you may be wondering why I'm contemplating selling them. Especially since I rated them a buy two months ago. Well, here's the ETF I'm debating swapping them out for and why.

Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (NYSEARCA:QDVO)

If you've followed me here, then you know I'm all about buying and holding companies or funds that pay dividends or distributions. And after doing some extensive research, QDVO is the ETF I've set my eyes on.

Unlike SCHD, QDVO has a much shorter track record, with an inception date of 08/22/2024, so there is limited history to go on. For some, this may make them riskier. They also have an entirely different strategy than SCHD by selling call options to generate income instead of holding quality dividend payers.

But here's what attracted me to QDVO vs other covered call funds. Although they have a much higher yield than SCHD due to them selling options, the ETF prioritizes growth first and income second.

Most covered call ETFs prioritize income first and growth second. And because of the options strategy, their capital appreciation is capped. So, QDVO's tactical strategy makes them better vs some peers in that aspect.

Additionally, QDVO is actively managed vs passively and has higher exposure to growth potential with most of its portfolio concentrated in technology stocks.

Their top holdings include behemoth NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA), which makes up 10.32% of the portfolio. Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN) round out the top 5. I also like that Visa (V) is included in its top 10 holdings.

I'm a huge fan of Visa's capital-light business model. They're also known as one of the best dividend growth stocks around. QDVO also holds Invesco's Short-Term Investments Trust Government & Portfolio Agency (AGPXX) to generate income.

I like the fact that the ETF prefers to hold underlying equities instead of using a synthetic options strategy like other covered call funds. This, in my opinion, makes them better to hold long-term and less likely to experience NAV & price erosion.

Although their track record is very short, their total returns have been stellar over the past 14 months. QDVO has significantly outperformed SCHD in total returns while also beating peers like JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) & NEOS' NASDAQ-100 High Income ETF (QQQI).

Below is a more detailed comparison of SCHD & QDVO and how I compared them in my decision process:

Distribution Yield

QDVO

12.17% vs. 3.88% at time of writing

Expense Ratio

SCHD

0.06% vs 0.55%

Payment Frequency

QDVO

monthly vs. quarterly

Tax Structure

SCHD

qualified vs. return of capital

Size

SCHD

$72.2B in net assets vs. 310.9M

Holdings

QDVO

more growth-focused vs. defensive

Style

QDVO

active vs. passive

2025 Avg Distribution

SCHD

$0.2585 vs. $0.2320

Both ETFs are tied, with QDVO taking the yield, payment frequency, style, and portfolio concentration sections. This makes them better for long-term compounding since they have a much higher yield, are concentrated in tech, and pay monthly vs. quarterly. Their active management strategy also allows them to rebalance more frequently if they underperform.

However, SCHD's lower cost structure ($6 for every $10k invested), larger size, favorable tax structure, and higher average distribution for this year are also attractive for long-term investors.

Return of capital is considered favorable as well since taxes are deferred until you sell or your cost basis reaches zero. However, I give the edge to SCHD since some of QDVO's distributions, although mostly ROC, do consist of net investment income.

And depending on your tax bracket, this can subject you to an additional 3.8% tax. Here's an example below:

Here's how you would be taxed depending on which category you fall into:

Risks & Conclusion

Although I'm considering selling SCHD for QDVO, investors should know that because of QDVO's covered call strategy, this caps their upside, whereas SCHD could see a better performance in a bull market if their underlying holdings experience significant upside.

Moreover, QDVO could experience NAV erosion, although their unique approach using a tactical options strategy has allowed the fund to see solid NAV growth since inception. But if you're considering buying, this is something to be aware of going forward.

SCHD, although I may sell, continues to do its job and could outperform over the long term as technology stocks could experience a correction or underperformance.

I still think the ETF has its place depending on an investor's strategy. But if you're contemplating selling, consider Amplify's CWP Growth & Income ETF as a suitable replacement.

data.title: The Nuclear Catalyst Behind Palantir's Surge
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) (NEOE:PLTR:CA) jumped almost 20% over the last month as momentum gathered around its growing influence across defense, manufacturing, and enterprise AI. September brought fresh catalysts, including a new UK defense partnership with Hadean, an expanded contract with Lear Corporation, and multiple customer wins highlighted at its AIPCon event.

Alongside its $100 million Nuclear Operating System (NOS) deal announced in June, these developments underscore Palantir's positioning as an operating system for sovereign and commercial infrastructure, fueling optimism ahead of upcoming earnings.

From Defense to Energy: Palantir's NOS Revolution

Palantir's new NOS shows how the company's mission is broadening beyond AI enablement to energy security. The stakes are geopolitical. China is installing 10 GW of nuclear reactors each year, while America's new capacity over thirty years totals only 2 GW.

The executive orders by President Trump in May stipulate 400 GW by the year 2050, with ten reactors under construction by the year 2030. By matching its software with America's new nuclear build-out, Palantir is positioning itself not only at the operating layer of defense and finance but also of energy itself.

Palantir collaborated with a nuclear construction company to design an artificial intelligence-based NOS, a five-year, $100 million contract with a mission to speed up nuclear build-outs. This stroke deposits Palantir between the nexus of two megatrends, the AI compute iteration and global energy transition.

The hyperscalers and nation-states alike invest hundreds of billions of dollars in data centers and GPUs, but the former is of no value without power. As electricity demand is being exponentially spurred by AI, scalable and reliable sources of energy are now the limiting factor of digital expansion. Nuclear power finds itself the only plausible baseload substitute to fuel this bulge.

NOS can address the challenge of building nuclears, synchronizing supply chains, regulation, safety, and financing. A success of Palantir will not only make it a software supplier to power utilities but also the operating layer that enables economically viable gigawatt-scale capacity of nuclears. That links the future of Palantir's growth to the future direction of AI energy demand.

This energy role expands its moat. Defense contracts make Palantir integral to national defense. The Treasury and the public make it integral to financial management. Energy deployments make it the core of the physical infrastructure supporting AI itself. Very few software businesses span such areas. Investors make this create an option value not reflected in conventional multiples; Palantir might turn out to be just as integral to nuclear and grid infrastructure as to defense.

From 442 GW to 515 GW: Palantir Embeds Into the Nuclear Expansion

The world nuclear power sector will keep growing gradually from 2025's 442.9 GW to over 515 GW by 2034, estimated by Precedence Research. The expansion is not only about diversification of energy but also about keeping up with the rising electricity appetite of data centers powering AIs and next-generation industrial infrastructure. Palantir's new NOS announced lately is aligned with this growth.

By inserting its software into nuclear building and operating, Palantir insures itself a seat on the operating tier of one of the world's quickest-growing pieces of the global energy mix. As nuclear capacity expands, the difficulty of aligning compliance, building schedules, supply chains, and safety procedures becomes a chokepoint. NOS specifically calls out these frictions and makes Palantir a key facilitator of gigawatt-scale growth.

Effectively, with the addition of ~73 GW of capacity to the nuclear power market over the coming decade, Palantir can seize repeat, infrastructure-grade revenue streams not correlated with a single sector but with the interning of energy that is integral to the AI era. The structural connection between the scalability of energy and demand within digital infrastructure gives a distinguishable vector of growth separate and apart from its core contracts within both defence and AI.

Why the Premium Stays

Near ~$180 per share, Palantir is trading close to 100x forward EV/Sales and above 216x forward EV/EBITDA. Such multiples seem absurd by traditional standards, but they endure because Palantir is no longer being compared to software peers. It is rather being traded as an option on sovereign life-or-death infrastructure.

Bank of America late last year increased its target to $215, specifically mentioning the secret sauce of agentic AI combined with forward-deployed engineers. That model is true and whole. What's being paid for is not only the speed of AI adoption but also the operating layer-ness of Palantir into systems that governments can't afford to have fail: defense, finance, immigration, and energy. Every new contract in these areas adds hardness, lengthening the interval of cash flows and supporting the premium.

Nevertheless, risks exist. Pressure to interoperate from regulators might require Palantir to open its architecture and dilute lock-in. Political backlash against contracts such as ImmigrationOS might affect global adoption. Expansion of its high-touch deployment model globally will challenge margin resilience.

Looking ahead, analysts expect Palantir to keep compounding growth while expanding earnings power. Estimates consolidate EPS of $0.64 in 2025, increasing by 33% to $0.85 in 2026 as revenues grow from $4.16 billion to $5.62 billion during the same years.

That means a still-hilly forward multiple (P/E ~287x for 2025, ~218x for 2026), but it captures optimism that Palantir will be able to maintain high double-digit top-line growth with operating leverage. With several quarters of consistent EPS beats and commercial deal momentum, expectations are rising that the company will continue converting its expanding pipeline into durable cash flows.

Risks to the Thesis

Palantir's course into civic and national infrastructure carries with it idiosyncratic risks that go beyond run-of-the-mill software execution. The first is the spotlight of regulation. As governments increasingly rely on Palantir to power their defense, financial, and immigration systems, regulators will insist on interoperability mandates to prevent vendor lock-in. Such mandates can erode Palantir's proprietary benefit by requiring that it expose its architecture, diluting switching costs that presently support its moat.

Political backlash is another key risk. The ImmigrationOS prototype showcases how readily Palantir can be its lightning rod during polarized political controversies over spying, privacy, and civil liberties. Global markets like the EU, with data protection heavily codified, might restrict adoption if Palantir can be viewed by its constituents as being too closely associated with contentious US initiatives.

Economically, scaling its globally forward-deployed engineer model operationally is not straightforward. Effective in shortening deployment cycles, it's capital-intensive. Lacking extensive automation, Palantir faces margin erosion with escalating customer volumes.

Lastly, valuation of the company implies lofty expectations. At ~100x EV/sales, even a small execution misstep within nuclear, Treasury, or defense initiatives might induce oversized market repricing. Essentially, the transformation of Palantir into sovereign infrastructure strengthens longevity but expands political, regulatory, and scaling risks.

Bottom Line

Palantir is transforming from an artificial intelligence vendor to sovereign-generic infrastructure, inscribing itself into defense, finance, and nuclear power, where displacement is not an option. The $100 million Nuclear Operating System anchors its growth to the artificial intelligence energy bottleneck, the characteristic constraint of the digital proliferation.

As nuclear capacity increases from 442 GW in 2025 to 515 GW by 2034, Palantir seizes infrastructure-grade revenues through necessary systems. This stability builds option value beyond software peers, supporting its premium and eventual upside.
